examples.title: Process Creation using fork()
examples.description: Demonstrates creating a child process in C using fork() system call
examples.explanation: fork() creates an identical copy of the process. The child gets PID=0 in its context, parent gets child's PID. Both continue execution from after fork(). Parent calls wait() to prevent zombie child.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is address space size

examples.title: Round Robin CPU Scheduling
examples.description: Implementation of Round Robin scheduling algorithm
examples.code:
def round_robin(processes, burst_time, quantum):
    n = len(processes)
    remaining_bt = burst_time.copy()  # Remaining burst time
    wait_time = [0] * n
    turnaround_time = [0] * n
    t = 0  # Current time
    
    # Process until all are done
    while True:
        done = True
        for i in range(n):
            if remaining_bt[i] > 0:
                done = False
                
                if remaining_bt[i] > quantum:
                    t += quantum
                    remaining_bt[i] -= quantum
                else:
                    t += remaining_bt[i]
                    wait_time[i] = t - burst_time[i]
                    remaining_bt[i] = 0
        
        if done:
            break
    
    # Calculate turnaround time
    for i in range(n):
        turnaround_time[i] = wait_time[i] + burst_time[i]
    
    # Print results
    print("Process\tBurst\tWaiting\tTurnaround")
    for i in range(n):
        print(f"P{i+1}\t{burst_time[i]}\t{wait_time[i]}\t{turnaround_time[i]}")

# Example
processes = [1, 2, 3, 4]
burst_time = [10, 5, 8, 12]
quantum = 4
round_robin(processes, burst_time, quantum)
examples.explanation: Round Robin gives each process equal time slice (quantum). If burst > quantum, process is preempted and goes to back of ready queue. Provides fair scheduling and good response time for interactive systems.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × q) where q is quantum, n is number of processes

examples.title: Semaphore Implementation and Usage
examples.explanation: Semaphore is an integer with atomic wait() and signal() operations. wait() decrements and blocks if value becomes 0. signal() increments and wakes blocked process. This ensures mutual exclusion (binary semaphore = mutex).
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for wait and signal operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(s) where s is number of semaphores

examples.title: SJF (Shortest Job First) Scheduling
examples.code:
def sjf_scheduling(processes, burst_time):
    n = len(processes)
    
    # Pair and sort by burst time
    pairs = list(zip(processes, burst_time))
    pairs.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])  # Sort by burst time
    
    waiting_time = [0] * n
    turnaround_time = [0] * n
    
    # First process has 0 waiting time
    waiting_time[0] = 0
    
    # Calculate waiting times
    for i in range(1, n):
        waiting_time[i] = pairs[i-1][1] + waiting_time[i-1]
    
    # Calculate turnaround times
    for i in range(n):
        turnaround_time[i] = waiting_time[i] + pairs[i][1]
    
    # Print results
    print("Process\tBurst\tWaiting\tTurnaround")
    for i in range(n):
        print(f"P{pairs[i][0]}\t{pairs[i][1]}\t{waiting_time[i]}\t{turnaround_time[i]}")
    
    avg_wait = sum(waiting_time) / n
    avg_turnaround = sum(turnaround_time) / n
    print(f"\nAverage Waiting Time: {avg_wait}")
    print(f"Average Turnaround Time: {avg_turnaround}")

# Example
processes = [1, 2, 3, 4]
burst_time = [6, 8, 7, 3]
sjf_scheduling(processes, burst_time)
examples.explanation: SJF selects the process with shortest burst time first. It provides optimal average waiting time but requires knowing burst times in advance. It can cause starvation of long processes. This is non-preemptive version.
